title: PHP Superglobals
content:
### PHP Superglobals

Superglobals are built-in variables that are always accessible, regardless of scope.

### Available Superglobals

| Variable | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `$GLOBALS` | References all global variables |
| `$_SERVER` | Server and execution environment info |
| `$_GET` | HTTP GET variables |
| `$_POST` | HTTP POST variables |
| `$_FILES` | HTTP File Upload variables |
| `$_COOKIE` | HTTP Cookies |
| `$_SESSION` | Session variables |
| `$_REQUEST` | GET, POST, and COOKIE combined |
| `$_ENV` | Environment variables |

### $_SERVER Examples

```php
<?php
echo $_SERVER["PHP_SELF"];     // Current script path
echo $_SERVER["SERVER_NAME"];  // Server hostname
echo $_SERVER["HTTP_HOST"];    // HTTP Host header
echo $_SERVER["REMOTE_ADDR"];  // Client IP address
echo $_SERVER["REQUEST_METHOD"]; // GET, POST, etc.
echo $_SERVER["HTTP_USER_AGENT"]; // Browser user agent
?>
```

### Accessing Superglobals

```php
<?php
// In any scope - no global keyword needed
function test() {
    echo $_SERVER["REQUEST_METHOD"]; // Works
    echo $_GET["id"] ?? "not set";   // Works
}
?>
```

> **Key Point:** Superglobals are available in all scopes without using the `global` keyword. They are populated automatically by PHP.
codeExample: "; echo "KeyValue"; $keys = ["PHP_SELF", "SERVER_NAME", "HTTP_HOST", "REMOTE_ADDR", "REQUEST_METHOD", "SCRIPT_NAME"]; foreach ($keys as $key) { echo "$key" . ($_SERVER[$key] ?? "N/A") . ""; } echo ""; // Check request method if ($_SERVER["REQUEST_METHOD"] === "POST") { echo "Form was submitted via POST"; } ?>

quiz.question: How many superglobal variables are there in PHP?
Do superglobals require the global keyword to be accessed inside functions?
